Party_Details.Name: W&P Piling Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: W&P Piling Pte Ltd was incorporated and engaged in mixed construction activities
The plaintiff purchased the IMT AF 18 hydraulic equipment for $740,000 on 1997-11-27
The plaintiff entered into a scheme of arrangement in January 2002 to pay its creditors in four cash instalments totalling 0.40¢ for every $1.00
The plaintiff sold the first machine to Wee Poh for $380,000 on or about 2002-05-02

Party_Details.Issue: Whether directors can be held legally responsible for transferring company assets without proper accounting
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must act in the best interest of the company and ensure proper accounting for asset transfers
Party_Details.Application: The directors breached their statutory and fiduciary duties by transferring the company's heavy machinery to the parent company at an undervalue and without proper accounting or valuation.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the directors failed to act in the company's best interest by transferring assets without proper valuation or accounting, leading to financial detriment.

Party_Details.Name: Chew Yin What

Facts.Text: Chew Yin What was a director of W&P Piling Pte Ltd from 9 May 1996 to 12 April 2003
He approved the sale of the first machine to Wee Poh on or about 2 May 2002 without valuation
He was involved in agreeing that Wee Poh would take over hire of the third and fourth machines from Caterpillar in April and May 2002
He responded on behalf of Wee Poh to Don Ho's demand for payment on 5 June 2003 and disputed the amount owed to the plaintiff
He signed a letter on behalf of Wee Poh on 27 June 2003 asserting that the adjudicated sum had already been included in the original sum owed to the plaintiff before set-offs

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director is legally responsible for failing to account for asset transfers and proceeds
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must ensure proper accounting and valuation of company assets during transfers
Party_Details.Application: The asset transfers were necessary commercial decisions executed in good faith to alleviate the company's severe cash flow problems and manage its debts, negating any dishonest intent.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that the Defendant failed to fulfill his duties by not ensuring proper valuation and accounting, resulting in financial harm to the company.

Party_Details.Name: Lee Kok Swee

Facts.Text: Lee Kok Swee was appointed as a director of W&P Piling Pte Ltd
Lee Kok Swee approved the sale of the first machine to Wee Poh, and no valuation was obtained before the sale
Lee Kok Swee concurred in the transfer of the five machines to Wee Poh, and the plaintiff did not receive the sale proceeds from those transactions

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director is legally responsible for failing to account for asset transfers and proceeds
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must ensure proper accounting and valuation of company assets during transfers
Party_Details.Application: The asset transfers were executed in good faith to manage the company's cash flow problems and were not carried out with any dishonest intent to defraud the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that the Defendant failed to fulfill his duties by not ensuring proper valuation and accounting, resulting in financial harm to the company.

Party_Details.Name: Yeung Chun Keung

Facts.Text: Yeung Chun Keung was a director of W&P Piling Pte Ltd from 1996-05-27 to 2003-04-12

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director can be held legally responsible for failing to prevent asset transfers without proper accounting
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must exercise due diligence to prevent unauthorized asset transfers
Party_Details.Application: As a mere nominee director, he had limited involvement in the company's daily operations and reasonably relied on the scheme administrator and other professionals to manage the company's financial difficulties.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court acknowledged the Defendant's limited role but found a failure to exercise due diligence by turning a blind eye to the actions of his co-directors.
